question: [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization struct with authorization.nonce set to a very large value (e.g., type(uint256).max - 1) to cause nonce[authorizer] to overflow to 0 after the next successful authorization, allowing replay of all previously consumed nonces? Preconditions: attacker has a valid signature for Authorization{nonce=type(uint256).max-1}; current nonce[authorizer] == type(uint256).max-1. Call sequence: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth with nonce=type(uint256).max-1) -> nonce[authorizer] becomes type(uint256).max -> next call with nonce=type(uint256).max -> nonce[authorizer] overflows to 0 -> all nonces from 0 to type(uint256).max-1 become replayable. Invariant tested: nonce must never overflow to enable replay. Scoped impact: gate bypass via nonce overflow replay. Proof idea: fuzz nonce values near type(uint256).max, assert overflow is impossible in Solidity 0.8.34 (checked arithmetic).

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize a contract that implements both IEnterGate and is an operator of a whitelisted user, then use that contract to simultaneously act as the gate (returning true for canIncreaseCredit/canIncreaseDebt for the attacker) and as an operator to call take on behalf of the whitelisted user, creating a circular authorization that bypasses the gate's intended restriction? Preconditions: gated market where the gate contract is also an authorized operator of a whitelisted user; attacker controls the gate contract. Call sequence: attacker deploys maliciousGateAndOperator -> whitelisted user authorizes it -> maliciousGateAndOperator.canIncreaseDebt(attacker) returns true -> attacker calls take(offer, ..., taker=attacker) -> gate passes for attacker. Invariant tested: gate contracts must not be exploitable as operators to bypass their own restrictions. Scoped impact: gate bypass via circular authorization. Proof idea: deploy gate-operator contract, assert attacker can access gated market.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then use Midnight.flashLoan to borrow loan tokens and use the onFlashLoan callback to call Midnight.take on behalf of the whitelisted user in a gated market, bypassing the gate check because the buyer is the whitelisted user? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator; attacker implements IFlashLoanCallback. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.flashLoan(tokens, amounts, attackerCallback, data) -> attackerCallback.onFlashLoan calls Midnight.take(offer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) -> passes. Invariant tested: gate checks must apply even during flash loan callbacks. Scoped impact: gate bypass via flash loan callback. Proof idea: implement flash loan callback that takes on behalf of whitelisted user, assert gate invariants.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker and the attacker as the takerCallback, using the onSell callback to receive loan tokens directly, effectively using the whitelisted user's gate access to borrow on behalf of the whitelisted user while the attacker receives the proceeds? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator; attacker implements ISellCallback. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(offer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser, takerCallback=attacker) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) -> passes -> attacker.onSell receives loan tokens. Invariant tested: gate bypass must not allow non-whitelisted actors to receive proceeds from gated market borrows. Scoped impact: gate bypass; unauthorized loan token receipt. Proof idea: unit test attacker as takerCallback receiving proceeds from gated market take.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker and receiverIfTakerIsSeller set to the attacker's address, so that when the whitelisted user sells units (increases debt), the loan tokens are sent directly to the attacker? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(offer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser, receiverIfTakerIsSeller=attacker) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) -> passes -> loan tokens sent to attacker. Invariant tested: gate bypass must not allow non-whitelisted actors to receive loan tokens from gated market borrows via receiver parameter. Scoped impact: gate bypass; unauthorized loan token receipt. Proof idea: unit test attacker as receiver in gated market take.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as maker (via a signed offer) and the attacker as taker, where the gate checks canIncreaseCredit(attacker) for the attacker as buyer, and the attacker is not whitelisted, causing the gate to block the take? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user is maker with a buy offer; attacker is taker; gate checks canIncreaseCredit(taker). Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.take(buyOffer with maker=whitelistedUser, ..., taker=attacker) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(attacker) -> attacker not whitelisted -> SellerGatedFromIncreasingDebt revert. Invariant tested: gate must check the actual buyer/seller addresses, not the operator. Scoped impact: gate correctly blocks non-whitelisted takers. Proof idea: confirm gate checks buyer/seller not operator in take function.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker in a market where the enterGate checks canIncreaseDebt based on the taker's address, and the whitelisted user's debt is already at maximum, causing the take to increase debt beyond the whitelisted user's intended limit while the attacker benefits? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator; whitelisted user has existing debt. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(sellOffer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) -> passes -> whitelistedUser's debt increases beyond their intent. Invariant tested: operator authorization must not enable debt increase beyond the authorizer's intent in gated markets. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt increase; potential undercollateralization. Proof idea: fuzz debt amounts, assert whitelisted user's debt does not exceed collateral capacity.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker in a post-maturity gated market where debt increase is forbidden, and the gate check passes for the whitelisted user but the CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity check should block the take? Preconditions: gated market; post-maturity; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> warp to post-maturity -> attacker calls Midnight.take(sellOffer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate checks canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) -> passes -> CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity check -> revert. Invariant tested: post-maturity debt increase must be blocked regardless of gate status or operator authorization. Scoped impact: gate bypass combined with maturity boundary violation. Proof idea: fuzz timestamp at maturity boundary, assert debt cannot increase post-maturity even via operator.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.liquidate on behalf of the whitelisted user (but liquidate has no onBehalf parameter), and does the liquidatorGate check canLiquidate(msg.sender) correctly use the attacker's address rather than the whitelisted user's address, preventing the gate bypass? Preconditions: market with liquidatorGate; whitelisted liquidator has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator of whitelisted liquidator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.liquidate(market, ...) as msg.sender -> liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(attacker) -> attacker not whitelisted -> LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating revert. Invariant tested: liquidator gate must check msg.sender, not any authorized operator. Scoped impact: liquidator gate correctly blocks non-whitelisted callers. Proof idea: confirm liquidate uses msg.sender for gate check, not onBehalf.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize a contract that implements ILiquidatorGate as an operator of a whitelisted user, and then have that contract call Midnight.liquidate where canLiquidate(msg.sender) is called on the liquidatorGate with msg.sender being the gate-operator contract, which returns true for itself, creating a circular bypass of the liquidator gate? Preconditions: market with liquidatorGate; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker deploys a contract that is both an operator and implements canLiquidate returning true for itself. Call sequence: attacker deploys selfWhitelistingContract -> whitelisted user authorizes it via EcrecoverAuthorizer -> selfWhitelistingContract calls Midnight.liquidate -> liquidatorGate.canLiquidate(selfWhitelistingContract) -> selfWhitelistingContract.canLiquidate returns true -> liquidation proceeds. Invariant tested: liquidator gate must not be bypassable by self-whitelisting operator contracts. Scoped impact: liquidator gate bypass; unauthorized liquidation. Proof idea: deploy self-whitelisting contract, assert it cannot bypass liquidator gate.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then use Midnight.multicall to batch multiple take calls on behalf of the whitelisted user in a single transaction, each increasing the whitelisted user's debt, and does the gate check apply to each individual take within the multicall? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.multicall([take(offer1, ..., taker=whitelistedUser), take(offer2, ..., taker=whitelistedUser)]) -> each take checks gate -> gate passes for whitelistedUser in each call. Invariant tested: gate checks must apply to each take within a multicall batch. Scoped impact: gate bypass via multicall batching; unauthorized debt accumulation. Proof idea: fuzz number of takes in multicall, assert gate is checked for each.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker and a reduceOnly=true offer, where the gate check for canIncreaseDebt is skipped because sellerDebtIncrease==0, allowing the attacker to reduce the whitelisted user's credit without gate restriction? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has credit; attacker becomes operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(reduceOnlyOffer with buy=true, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> sellerDebtIncrease==0 -> gate check for canIncreaseDebt skipped -> whitelistedUser's credit reduced. Invariant tested: gate must not be bypassable via reduceOnly offers that reduce credit without increasing debt. Scoped impact: unauthorized credit reduction; potential fund loss. Proof idea: test reduceOnly take via operator, assert gate behavior for credit reduction.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with units=0 (a no-op take) to trigger the maker's callback without any gate check (since buyerCreditIncrease==0 and sellerDebtIncrease==0), and use the callback to perform gate-restricted actions on behalf of the whitelisted user? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator; maker has a callback that performs gate-restricted actions. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(offer with callback, units=0, taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate checks skipped (no credit/debt increase) -> maker callback executes gate-restricted action. Invariant tested: zero-unit takes must not bypass gate checks for callback-triggered actions. Scoped impact: gate bypass via zero-unit take with callback. Proof idea: test zero-unit take with gate-restricted callback, assert gate invariants.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker in a market where the enterGate's canIncreaseCredit function has a side effect (e.g., updating a whitelist based on the caller), and the gate is called with the whitelisted user's address, causing the gate to update its state in a way that inadvertently whitelists the attacker? Preconditions: gated market with stateful enterGate; whitelisted user has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker becomes operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> attacker calls Midnight.take(buyOffer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate.canIncreaseCredit(whitelistedUser) called -> gate updates state -> attacker becomes whitelisted. Invariant tested: gate state updates must not be exploitable via operator-mediated takes. Scoped impact: gate bypass via stateful gate side effects. Proof idea: deploy stateful gate, assert attacker cannot become whitelisted via operator-mediated take.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Gate or ratifier bypass allowing restricted markets, restricted liquidation, or restricted credit/debt increase to be accessed by unauthorized users'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves as an operator of a whitelisted gate user, then call Midnight.take with the whitelisted user as taker in a market where the enterGate reverts for the whitelisted user (e.g., the gate was updated to block the whitelisted user), and does the revert correctly propagate to block the take? Preconditions: gated market; whitelisted user was previously whitelisted but gate now reverts for them; attacker is operator. Call sequence: attacker gains operator status -> gate updated to revert for whitelistedUser -> attacker calls Midnight.take(offer, ..., taker=whitelistedUser) -> gate.canIncreaseDebt(whitelistedUser) reverts -> take reverts. Invariant tested: gate reverts must propagate correctly even for operator-mediated takes. Scoped impact: gate correctly blocks reverted gate checks. Proof idea: test gate revert propagation for operator-mediated takes.
